# Transcrição: 06-Reduce CPM via Ad Quality Score Analysis

- **Fonte:** 06-Reduce CPM via Ad Quality Score Analysis
- **Duração:** 10:34

## [00:00 - 03:01]

when it comes to looking at your ad level performance, the click-through rate, the cost per click, the CPMs, the conversion rates, the rise, they're all great indicators and they are front-facing indicators into what your performance is like. But how do you know how your ad is performing for a given audience? What the kind of feedback that Facebook is seeing in the auction itself? So we talk about the auction and the inputs and how Facebook look at your campaigns and your ads and your account and grade you in terms of cost and placement. But one of the things that Facebook also provide is insight as to what they see as an output of your ad in the auction. So what they used to do was to give you a relevant score. So that score would be out of 10 and it would just broadly tell you whether the ad performed well in the auction or not. So high score would be that you ended up with the lowest auction costs and best users, et cetera. So the goal was always to get your relevant score as high as possible. But that said, I was still scaling ad accounts to tens of thousands of spend in a day with low relevant scores. So my relevant scores would sometimes end up being three or four out of 10. You know, one of the things about this is the relevant score and the kind of quality metrics in the auction, they don't dictate what happens with your business. All it is telling you is that the cost and the quality of your auction wins could be better. So for example, when I was scaling and seeing those low relevant scores years ago, I would look at that as an indicator. I wouldn't look at that as, oh, damn, my relevant scores are too low. I need to optimize and change my spend and do something. No, your first optimization is whatever your goal metric is. So cost per acquisition, cost per lead, cost per reg, return on ad spend, whatever your North Star metric is, that's the thing you generally focus on. That's your KPI, Keep Performance Indicator. But just like when you're driving a car, you have a dashboard and you have lights and alerts that come up to tell you that something's not quite right. And that's what the ad quality scores are about. So Facebook introduced a breakdown of the relevant score, I think maybe late 2018 into 2019, as a way of giving you more insight. And there's three main metrics that you need to keep an eye on which are your quality ranking, your engagement ranking, and also your conversion rate ranking. There are three core parts of the auction. And what Facebook's telling you is once your ad has been through the auction, how it came out on the other side.

## [03:01 - 06:02]

So for example, this ad here, which had the most spend of all these ads that were running, the conversion rate ranking is looking at how people that click the ad performed versus ads that they were also seeing elsewhere. So the conversion rate ranking is based on the user. So the user has seen my ad and they haven't converted it as well on my ad as they have on other people's ad. The engagement rate ranking is then looking at how the, if you go back to the auction and you look at the estimated action rate, the engagement rate ranking is looking at how relevant my ad was for the audience in terms of matching my avatar. So if you talk about angles and avatars and grad testing and trying to find ads that resonate, there's something here, again, that's below average. And then the kind of quality ranking is overall, what kind of quality score Facebook has given based on the performance. So on the one hand, they're looking at audience. On one side, they're looking at your user targeting and kind of how the user reacting and how engagement looks. And then the conversion rate. So how do you get quality ranking up? You improve your targeting and you get better performance from your ads. Get engagement ranking up is how your ads are performing in the auction at the user level. And then conversion rate ranking is really looking at your funnel. So Facebook by doing this are giving you feedback on three different levels. How is your audience targeting? How's your user engagement? And how's your funnel performing? Now you might look at this and say, well, this isn't looking great. There's a lot of averages below average, for example. But on this ad account, a $40 CPA is kind of good. So if I'm looking at this and I'm looking down my stats, yes, some of the ads are not performing as well as others. But if I look at this top spending ad in this period, $38 CPA, I'm gonna look at these metrics here as an indication of how I can improve that $38, but not to dictate what I do with my budget because I can continue scaling this. At $38 CPA, this ad is performing within my target and I can continue scaling. But what I would be looking at is, I'm looking at these metrics to determine, A, could I get my costs lower, my CPMs? And B, could I get the quality of users higher based on if these scores are high, then I've got a higher chance of reaching better people in my audience. So if you look at targeting and you say, my audience is 20 million people, I'm targeting purchases, et cetera, just because you're able to spend high and you're able to target wide, does not mean you're gonna reach the best users. Facebook have a quality control in the auction that they're trying to maintain. So that really dictates whether you can reach

## [09:03 - 10:34]

to determine what your problem is and what you can do to solve it. So ad quality scores, you should absolutely keep an eye on. They're there to help you optimize your performance to get better quality users at lower costs from the auction. And it's there to be used, the way I would look at this is every two weeks, you're checking on your scores, depending on how much you're spending. If you're spending below, I would say if you're doing below 20, 30K a month in ad spend, doing a check every two weeks is probably okay to just keep an eye on how performance is. As you start to step up beyond 30K a month and you talk about $1,000 a day upwards, then I would maybe check a bit more frequently. But essentially, let's say you're doing 10K a day ad spend, you wanna be checking this a bit more frequently, maybe once a week. If you're going 50K above, then maybe once or twice a week. Because what you're trying to do is optimize and tweak your performance, because as you're scaling, you want stability, you want profitability. And that comes from getting more and more auction wins. So this is where it becomes more and more critical, or even when you're starting out. And I see people that go from the grad testing stage and they don't necessarily always see the performance that they were hoping for. Come and have a look at your ad quality scores and see if there's something you're missing here that is causing you to potentially lose auctions or be charged more than you should.
